question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: setTotalSupply()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted token dissociate transaction with a negative totalSupply delta that, when aggregated via the `totalSupply = totalSupply == null ? newTotalSupply : totalSupply + newTotalSupply` branch, produces a history row with a supply value inconsistent with the actual on-chain sequence, causing auditors and downstream consumers to reconstruct a false token supply timeline?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: setTotalSupply()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger repeated token dissociate events on a deleted token to accumulate negative totalSupply increments in AbstractToken, causing the token_history table to record a supply trajectory that diverges from the canonical ledger order, effectively rewriting the visible mint/burn history without moving funds?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: setTotalSupply()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the null-check bypass in setTotalSupply() — where a null newTotalSupply causes an early return — to submit a partial token update that leaves the history row with a stale totalSupply, making the history table show a supply value from a prior epoch as if it were current?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: setTotalSupply()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a sequence of mint and dissociate-of-deleted-token events so that the UpsertColumn coalesce expression `case when {0} >= 0 then {0} else e_{0} + coalesce({0}, {1}) end` produces a totalSupply in the current token row that does not match any single on-chain state, corrupting the history continuity visible to block explorers?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.timestampRange] Can an unprivileged external user submit a token update transaction whose consensus timestamp is earlier than an existing token_history row's timestampRange lower bound, causing the upsert logic to insert an out-of-order history entry that makes the token's state-change sequence appear reordered to any consumer querying token_history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.timestampRange] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a token operation that produces a timestampRange with an upper bound equal to its lower bound (empty range), causing the history row to be silently dropped or misinterpreted by range-query consumers, creating a gap in the visible transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.timestampRange] Can an unprivileged external user submit rapid successive token updates within the same consensus second so that multiple history rows share overlapping timestampRange values, causing range-based history queries to return duplicate or ambiguous state snapshots that misrepresent the true transaction order?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.timestampRange] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Range<Long> open/closed boundary semantics of timestampRange to craft a token update where the new range's lower bound equals the previous range's upper bound, causing a history row to appear as if two distinct state changes happened at the same instant, reordering the visible history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.createdTimestamp] Can an unprivileged external user submit a token creation event with a crafted createdTimestamp that is later than an existing token update's consensus timestamp, causing the history table to show a token that was 'updated before it was created', breaking the causal ordering of the token's history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.createdTimestamp] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Column(updatable = false)` annotation on createdTimestamp — which prevents JPA updates but may not block native upsert SQL — to inject a token history row with a falsified createdTimestamp that reorders the token's apparent creation relative to other tokens?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.freezeStatus] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Enumerated(EnumType.ORDINAL)` storage of freezeStatus to submit a token event that stores an unexpected integer ordinal, causing the history row to display a freeze state that never existed on-chain, corrupting the freeze/unfreeze history sequence?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.kycStatus] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Enumerated(EnumType.ORDINAL)` storage of kycStatus to inject a history row with an ordinal value that maps to a different enum constant than intended, making the token's KYC history appear to show a revoke/grant sequence that never occurred?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.pauseStatus] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the inconsistency between `@Enumerated(EnumType.ORDINAL)` used for freezeStatus/kycStatus and `@Enumerated(EnumType.STRING)` used for pauseStatus to craft a token event that stores a string value not matching any TokenPauseStatusEnum constant, causing a deserialization error that corrupts the pause history row?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.pauseStatus] Can an unprivileged external user submit a token update that sets pauseStatus to a string value with different casing than the enum constant (e.g., 'paused' vs 'PAUSED'), bypassing the named enum type check and inserting a history row with an unrecognized pause state that breaks the pause/unpause history sequence?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.supplyType] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Column(updatable = false)` constraint on supplyType — which is enforced at the JPA layer but not necessarily at the native SQL upsert layer — to inject a token_history row with a different supplyType than the token's actual type, making the history show a type change that never occurred?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.type] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Column(updatable = false)` annotation on the token type field to insert a history row via the native upsert path that shows a different TokenTypeEnum (e.g., NON_FUNGIBLE_UNIQUE vs FUNGIBLE_COMMON), rewriting the token's type history without changing the current state?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.initialSupply] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Column(updatable = false)` constraint on initialSupply to inject a token_history row with a falsified initialSupply value, causing history consumers to reconstruct a different token creation supply than what was actually set, corrupting the supply audit trail?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.maxSupply] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Column(updatable = false)` annotation on maxSupply to insert a history row with a maxSupply of 0 or Long.MAX_VALUE, making the token's historical supply cap appear different from its actual cap and misleading supply-limit enforcement checks in downstream systems?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.decimals] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Column(updatable = false)` constraint on decimals to inject a token_history row with a different decimal precision, causing historical balance calculations performed by downstream consumers to produce incorrect values and misrepresent the token's denomination history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.freezeDefault] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Column(updatable = false)` annotation on freezeDefault to inject a history row with freezeDefault=true when the actual token was created with freezeDefault=false, making the token's creation-time freeze policy appear different in history than it actually was?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.treasuryAccountId] Can an unprivileged external user submit a token update that sets treasuryAccountId to a different EntityId, causing a new history row to be written with the old treasury, and then immediately revert it, creating a history sequence that shows a treasury change that was never visible on-chain for any meaningful duration?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: AbstractToken.treasuryAccountId] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EntityIdConverter used for treasuryAccountId to inject a history row with a null or zero EntityId, causing the history table to show a period where the token had no treasury, which never existed on-chain, corrupting the treasury assignment history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: setName()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a token update with a name that, after DomainUtils.sanitize() processing, becomes identical to a different token's name, causing the history table to show a name collision that makes it appear two distinct tokens shared the same name at some point in history?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: setName()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a token name update with Unicode control characters or zero-width spaces that survive DomainUtils.sanitize() and are stored in the history row, causing history display tools to render the name differently than the actual on-chain name, creating a misleading history entry?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/domain/token/Token.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: setName()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a token name update with a null value that bypasses the DomainUtils.sanitize() call (since sanitize may return null for null input), causing a history row to be written with a null name that makes the history appear to show a name deletion event that never occurred on-chain?
